coredata.dc:title: Cooperation of carbon doping and carbon loading boosts photocatalytic activity by the optimum photo-induced electron trapping and interfacial charge transfer

coredata.dc:description: Carbon-based materials with various advantages have gained growing research interests in photocatalytic field, in which, employing carbon materials as active catalysts presents new opportunities for low-cost, green and sustainable energy conversion strategic. The performance of these carbon-based photocatalyst highly depends on several factors, including photoexcitation, charge separation and transfer and surface catalytic reaction, and surface catalytic process for H2 production. Here, the graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) system with doping carbon and loading carbon dots (CDs) was constructed via a direct thermal polymerization route to reveal the effect of trap states and displayed a 4-fold boosted H2 evolution efficiency relative to ordinary g-C3N4 during the photocatalysis. Transient absorption spectroscopy (TAS) results disclosed that the C-doping induced shallow trap states could capture photo-induced electrons to restrain deep trapping and direct recombination of photo-generated carriers. Density functional theory (DFT) calculation and transient photovoltage technique (TPV) test results confirmed that the fast holes’ transfer path established between CDs and C-doping g-C3N4 (CCN) make the CDs possess the extractive effect for the holes for the oxidation reaction with (TEOA), achieving spatial separation of electron-hole pairs. This study of interfacial charge transfer and transport dynamics provides a reference significance for the design and development of highly active carbon-based composite photocatalysts.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon-based materials with various advantages have gained growing research interests in photocatalytic field, in which, employing carbon materials as active catalysts presents new opportunities for low-cost, green and sustainable energy conversion strategic. The performance of these carbon-based photocatalyst highly depends on several factors, including photoexcitation, charge separation and transfer and surface catalytic reaction, and surface catalytic process for H2 production. Here, the graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) system with doping carbon and loading carbon dots (CDs) was constructed via a direct thermal polymerization route to reveal the effect of trap states and displayed a 4-fold boosted H2 evolution efficiency relative to ordinary g-C3N4 during the photocatalysis. Transient absorption spectroscopy (TAS) results disclosed that the C-doping induced shallow trap states could capture photo-induced electrons to restrain deep trapping and direct recombination of photo-generated carriers. Density functional theory (DFT) calculation and transient photovoltage technique (TPV) test results confirmed that the fast holes’ transfer path established between CDs and C-doping g-C3N4 (CCN) make the CDs possess the extractive effect for the holes for the oxidation reaction with (TEOA), achieving spatial separation of electron-hole pairs. This study of interfacial charge transfer and transport dynamics provides a reference significance for the design and development of highly active carbon-based composite photocatalysts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic conversion of solar-to-hydrogen (H2) can effectively overcome the disadvantages of conventional H2 production routes from finite fossil fuels (coal and oil), which is a promising approach to address the energy crisis and environmental problems [1]. Carbon-based materials with various advantages, such as rich source, tunable molecular structure, excellent photoelectrochemical property, high thermal stability, and environmental compatibility, have gained growing research interests in photocatalytic field [2]. Synchronously, it is more conducive to H2 evolution reaction (HER) under visible light for carbon-based photocatalysts with narrow band gap and slower charge recombination in the active center on their surface compared with other semiconductor materials (TiO2, ZnO, etc.) with broad-band gaps [3,4]. Furthermore, coupling carbon-based materials with carbonaceous materials (e.g., carbon dots, carbon nanotubes and graphene) has been demonstrated as an effective strategy to improve the photocatalytic performance owing to the outstanding optoelectronic properties of carbonaceous materials and intimate interfacial interaction between the two π-conjugated systems [5,6]. Consequently, heterogeneous photocatalysis employing carbon materials as active catalysts presents a rare opportunity for low-cost, green and sustainable energy conversion strategic. Typical photocatalytic reaction mainly includes photoexcitation, charge separation and transfer and surface catalytic reaction [7], and surface catalytic process for H2 production consists of the initial state of the electron and proton (e− + H+ → H*), the intermediate (H* adsorption) and the formation of final product (1/2 H2). In this regard, the lifetime of the photoexcited charge (ps-ns) and surface reaction time scale (ms-s), the initial state of the catalytic reaction could be directly determined by surface effective electron concentration, and the excited state carrier relaxation rate is the basic cause of the electron concentration, also indicates the premise of the final catalytic reaction efficiency dynamics factor [8]. However, whether the introduction of carbonaceous materials affects the carrier dynamics of the HER is rarely discussed. Meanwhile, the possibility of carbonaceous materials serving as co-catalysts for HER have not been ignored [9]. Consequently, a comprehensive investigation on the contributions of carbonaceous materials in carbonaceous/carbon-based composite photocatalysts is highly desired.

Of the classical carbon-based photocatalytic materials, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has triggered extensive studies since it was first reported to achieve H2 evolution under visible light in 2009, which contributes to its appropriate visible-light absorption, easy manufacturing, low-cost and chemical stability [10]. Unfortunately, the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4 is limited by the narrow absorption range of visible light, recombination of ultrafast charge carriers, deep trap state (DTS) capture of active electrons [11]. Interestingly, modification strategy based on tubular g-C3N4 material for dual-channels charge separation with doping S element and coating carbon dots (CDs) was developed and recently reported by us, resulting in photo-induced electrons with an extended lifetime [12]. Compared with heteroatom doping including B, P, S with the introduction of impurity, homogeneous C atom doping not only has the same effect of modulating the band structure and extending optical absorption range, but also bring delocalized big π bonds in favor of electron transfer [13]. Subsequently, the separated free photo-induced carriers can be transferred to the surface of photocatalysts to react with molecules directly, but some of them are likely to be captured by some intermediate states (trap states) between the quasi-Fermi levels of the electrons and holes, thus limiting the splitting of the quasi-Fermi level and dominating the migration and recombination process of photogenerated carriers [14]. According to the different energy difference of the capture state and the conduction band edge (ΔE), the trapped electrons are divided into shallow and deep trapped electrons [15]. The deeper the trapping state of the electron, the higher the required positive reduction potential, which means most of the electron energy loss, the lower the driving force of the catalytic reaction, which greatly limits the formation of the target product [16]. Consequently, it is valuable to develop an effective modification strategy to enhance the shallow capture of active electrons as much as possible and to inhibit the formation of deep capture electrons for further boosting the photocatalytic activity of C-doped g-C3N4. Recently, as one of the most representative carbon-based co-catalysts, CDs have received much attention due to their simple preparation, high Earth-abundance and excellent intrinsic properties, and the main function in CN-based photocatalyst systems is to improve the charge surface separation efficiency [17]. In addition to the superior optical absorption properties, CDs have been exhibited to be co-catalytic active sites and acceptor materials for electrons or holes, simultaneously generating H+ or electrons [18,19]. However, most studies have focused on the photochemical and photophysical processes of electrons, ignoring the influence of CDs on the level of charge carrier trapping. Thus, it is meaningful to deeply investigate transformation of electrons between different capture states and the synergism of carbon doping and carbon loading based on interfacial charge transfer and transport dynamics insight of dual-carbon species.

Herein, we built dual-carbon modified g-C3N4 system (CDs/CCN) with doping carbon and loading carbon dots (CDs) through a thermal polymerization strategy for highly enhanced photocatalytic H2 evolution. The as-prepared optimum CDs/CCN material exhibits excellent hydrogen yield up to 8682 μmol h−1 g−1, which is higher than the CN and CCN samples. Based on density functional theory (DFT) calculation, transient photovoltage technique (TPV) and transient absorption spectroscopy (TAS), the relationship between the electron trapping states and carbon doping and carbon loading in CN, interfacial charge transfer and transport dynamics insight and the directional extraction process of charge carriers were further investigated in detail.
Urea (CH4N2O, 99%) and gelatin were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd.

Carbon dots (CDs) solution was firstly prepared through the typical electrochemical method according to our previous report [20]. Briefly, two graphite rods were inserted into ultrapure water serving as an anode and a cathode, and a bias of 30 V was applied between two electrodes using a direct current power supply. After continuous stirring for 10 days, the dark black solution was formed in the reaction cell. Then, the dark solution was filtered and freeze-dried into powder for further use.

10 g urea was added in ceramic crucible and heated in muffle furnace at 550 °C for 2 h (heating rate: 5 °C/min). After colling to room temperature, the obtained light-yellow g-C3N4 sample was ground in agate mortar, collected, and named as CN.

Firstly, 100 mg gelatin was dispersed in 100 mL deionized water and stirred in water-bath with 60 °C for 30 min. Then, 10 g urea was added in 50 mL beakers with a certain amount of water (19, 17, 15 and 10 mL), and different amount of gelatin solution (1, 3, 5 and 10 mL) were poured in the corresponding beaker, respectively (the total amount of water in each beaker was 20 mL). Next, the above solution was stirred in water bath with 60 °C for 20 min to mix completely. After that, the prepared solution was poured in ceramic crucible and heated at 550 °C in muffle furnace for 2 h (heating rate: 5 °C/min). Finally, after colling to room temperature, the obtained samples were ground in agate mortar and collected. The abbreviation of the as-prepared C-doping CN samples was defined as CCN-x, and the × represents the additive amount of gelatin solution during stirring process. For example, CCN-5 represents that the additive amount of gelatin solution in the process of synthesis was 5 mL. On this basis, different mass ratios of CCN-1, CCN-3 and CCN-10 were prepared.

The procedure synthetic steps of CDs/CCN composite are given in Fig. 1 a. First of all, 10 mg CDs was dispersed in 10 mL deionized water and stirred on magnetic stirrer to form CDs solution at a known concentration. The synthetic process of y-CDs/CCN-5 was similar to that of CCN-5, expect for adding different volume of CDs solution (0.5, 1 and 2 mL) during the stirring process in water-bath, and y represents the additive volume of CDs solution. For example, 1-CDs/CCN-5 represents the additive volume of CDs in this sample was 1 mL. On this basis, different mass ratios of 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 and 2-CDs/CCN-5 were prepared.

Corresponding basic characterizations, detailed experimental procedures for photo-electrochemical tests, photocatalytic hydrogen production tests and density functional theory (DFT) calculations are provided in the Supporting Information.

The morphology and microstructure of as-prepared samples were analyzed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis methods. As shown in Fig. S1, the pure CN photocatalyst prepared from urea is a typical nanosheet structure. The CCN samples (Fig. S2) exhibit an open structure assembled by honeycomb nanostructure with a certain gap between the nanosheet because of CN polymerization with gas production and escape, and the addition of biopolymer gelatin can produce a certain amount of gas and a transient high internal pressure, leading to CN nanosheet coiling [21,22]. Note that the excessive addition of the gelatin ratio leads to the collapse of the honeycomb open structure (Fig. S2d). As given in Fig. S3a and b, the typical image of CDs reveals monodispersed nanocrystals with the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) crystal plane of graphite carbon [23]. In addition, according to Fig. S3c and d, an average diameter of CDs is about 3–7 nm and possess a significant UV–visible light response range. According to Fig. S4, the introduction of CDs in the composite could not significantly change the structure and morphology of CCN. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of CN (Fig. 1b), CCN (Fig. 1c) and 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 (Fig. 1d) can more intuitively see that the CCN and 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 still present the coiled honeycomb-like network structure compared to the CN, demonstrating that CDs have little effect on the morphology of CCN. Further observation of high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) of 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 in Fig. 1e present that the CDs are evenly distributed on the surface of the honeycomb structure-like CCN. According to the inset of Fig. 1e and Fig. S5, the crystallinity of CN and CCN still remain amorphous state and a significant lattice spacing of 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 of 0.21 nm is consistent with the carbon (001) crystal surface [24], which is preliminarily speculated that CDs were introduced in the CDs/CCN composite system. The high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-SEM) and element mapping images of 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 are provided in Fig. 1f, and the available sample contain C, N, and O elements and evenly distributed [25]. In addition, according to the elemental content obtained by energy dispersion X-ray spectrum (EDS) in Fig. S6, the C element content of CCN-5 and 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 increased significantly with the loading of CDs. The above characterization results confirm the initial success of the carbon doping and carbon loading in CN modification system.

To explore the details of the effects for carbon doping and carbon loading on the electronic structures and electronic properties of CN-based photocatalysts, the optimal C-doping sites of CN, CCN and CDs/CCN and performed the density functional theory (DFT) calculation on the optimized catalyst structures were analyzed. Considering the incomplete polymerization of CN, the following calculations were performed using the melon-type CN in which the heptazine unit is connected by a bridging amino group and the formed chain form a plane by hydrogen bonding [55]. In the atomic dopant system, the external atoms have three most likely doping sites in the mesh structure of CN (N1, N2 and N3) [56], and the possible models of single C atom substitution for N atom in CCN-5 are displayed in Fig. S9. After calculations, the binding energies of C atoms replacing N1, N2 and N3 were 0.896, −0.56 and 0.55 eV, respectively, with the introduction of C atoms most likely replacing the N1 site since N1 is the lowest. Hence, the calculated the charge density, band structure and density of states for three different catalysts were provided based on above optimized model (Fig. S10). Fig. 3 exhibits the charge density distributions of CN, CCN, and CDs/CCN, where the blue and yellow regions indicate charge accumulation and charge loss, respectively. It can be seen that electrons can be enriched and surrounded in the regions of N atoms and C-N bonds (Fig. 3a), and the random electrons distribution of the central N atoms can be enriched around the central C atom of the modified CCN, forming the local regions with higher electron density after the substitution of the central N atoms. In general, since the electronegativity of N atoms is stronger than that of C atoms, a certain trapped state could be formed during the doping process [57]. From the Fig. 3b, the C atoms around the doped C atoms in CCN more easily capture the electrons of the surrounding N atoms and form the electron-enriched region, which further confirms that the introduction of the internal C-species significantly changes the electronic structure and properties of CN in the volume, enabling electron localization around the C-doping site. Unlike the effect of C atomic doping, the introduced CDs only loaded on the surface and thus cannot change the mesh structure of the CN. As displayed in Fig. 3c, the vast majority of electrons are still enriched around the C atoms of the substrate material, and only a small number of electrons can be transferred to the surface of CDs. Different from CCN (Fig. 3b), the charge density of the substrate in CDs/CCN increases significantly, confirming that the main function of CDs is hole extraction, which not only effectively reduces the recombination rate of electron-hole pairs, but also realizes the directional migration of electrons and holes. The band structures based on the DFT calculations for the CN, CCN and CDs/CCN are given in Fig. 3d–f and the corresponding density of states (DOS) are presented in Fig. 3g–i. The computing results are much smaller compared with the actual measurements (Fig. 2h) due to the neglect of the self-interaction during the calculation of Perdew-Burke-Ernzerh (PBE) function [58]. According to the changing trend of band gaps, the band gap value of CCN significantly reduces CN compared with that of loading CDs, confirming that C atom doping is the main reason for significantly shortened band gap and structure optimization. Meanwhile, CCN and CDs/CCN have stronger linear density in the band maps in comparison of pristine, implying that more electrons can be generated to participate in the photocatalytic reaction [59].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic H2 evolution and cyclical stability
ce:section.ce:para: Apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) value of 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 sample at the wavelength of 420 nm. (d) Cycling stability tests for 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 sample. (e) Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution performance for 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 composite in comparison with other CN-based photocatalysts that reported in recent years.

The photocatalytic hydrogen evolution performance of CN, CCN-5, 0.5-CDs/CN and 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 photocatalysts was evaluated by using 25 mg of catalyst in an aqueous solution containing 10 vol% triethanolamine (TEOA) as sacrificial agent and 1 wt% Pt as co-catalyst. As displayed in Fig. 4 a and Fig. S11a, the hydrogen production of pristine CN reached 4212 μmol/g within 2 h irradiation, and the photocatalytic H2 production for CCN-5 (11286 μmol/g), 0.5-CDs/CN (7157 μmol/g) and 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 (17364 μmol/g) composites were significantly enhanced compared to that of bare CN. In terms of hydrogen production rate (Fig. 4b and Fig. S11b), CCN-5, 0.5-CDs/CN and 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 exhibit the outstanding H2 production rates of 5643 μmol h−1 g−1, 3579 μmol h−1 g−1 and 8682 μmol h−1 g−1, which were 2.68, 1.7 and 4.12 times better compared to that of the pristine CN, respectively. In Fig. 4c, the apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) value was recorded at the wavelength of 420 nm, and optimal 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 sample achieves an AQE value of up to 1.41%, which is better than other CN-based photocatalysts reported in recent years (Fig. 4e and Table S5) [60]. The experimental results for the cycle reaction in Fig. 4d demonstrates that there is no significant decrease in the hydrogen production performance of the as-prepared 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 photocatalyst. Meanwhile, the XRD patterns, FT-IR and UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. S12), SEM image (Fig. S13) and HRTEM images (Fig. S14) before and after the reaction are basically the same, indicating that the microstructure of sample after photocatalysis has not been destroyed, which again confirms the excellent cycling stability of 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 composite photocatalyst. To verify the potential influence of microstructure on photocatalytic hydrogen production performance, the Bruno-Emmett-teller (BET) specific surface area and pore size distribution of the prepared samples were obtained using the N2 adsorption–desorption isothermal method. As exhibited in Fig. S15, all samples exhibited type IV isotherms and H3 hysteresis loops, indicating the presence of mesoporous structures [61,62], and the corresponding mean size distributions are shown in Fig. S16. In addition, the BET specific surface areas of CN, CCN-5 and 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 are measured to be 42.3, 65.5 and 79.5 m2/g, respectively. The increase in the specific surface area of CCN-5 over CN can be explained by the gas impingement generated during the thermal polymerization process promoting the porous structure of CN [62–64], while he small increase in specific surface area obtained after the introduction of CDs in CDs/CCN due to its inherent properties of CDs, which provide abundant active sites during photocatalysis, facilitating the rapid diffusion of reactants and product molecules between porous networks[65].

The microscopic charge separation and transfer properties of photocatalysts were explored by steady-state photoluminescence (PL) and time-resolved transient photoluminescence (TRPL) decay curves. As exhibited in Fig. 5 a, the main PL emission peaks of all samples approximately station at 465 nm. In contrast to CN, the intensities of PL peaks for CCN-5 and 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 are sharply weakened and red-shifted, the former is due to the carbon doping and carbon loading modifications of the pristine CN to obtain superior charge separation characteristics, and the latter results from the incorporation of C = C organic components into the heptazine unit, resulting in the expansion of the π electron conjugate system [66], which also be confirmed by the UV–Vis DRS results (Fig. 2g). Furthermore, the TRPL decay curves in Fig. 5b can be fitted (Eq.1) according to the tri-exponential function in the following equation (τ) [67]:
(1)
t =
∑
A n
τ n 2
A n
τ n

The fitted results present that the single-linear exciton lifetimes of 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 (7.65 ns) and CCN-5 (8.01 ns) are significantly higher than those of CN (7.34 ns), meaning that electrons can be stored in the excited state to increase the possibility of charge separation, and low electron-hole recombination leads to higher lone pair electron concentration [68]. Based on the above analyses, with the doping of C atoms and the loading of CDs, 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 photocatalyst is an ideal photocatalyst with wide spectral response and abundant active site, as well as fast charge separation efficiency and activation energy level for the strong photocatalytic hydrogen production performance. As displayed in Fig. 5c and d, the photocurrent profile of 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 is significantly higher than that of CCN-5 and much higher than that of CN, and the EIS plot of 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 possesses the smallest impedance spectral radius among samples, indicating that the doping of C atoms and the decoration of CDs in CDs/CCN can not only improve the reduction potential of CN, greatly increasing the photocurrent response and generating more electrons to participate in the photocatalytic H2 evolution reaction [69], but also extend the advantage of small impedance to effectively improve the charge transfer capacity of CN. Typically, the complete photocatalytic hydrogen production process consists of three aspects: (i) the formation of H* (atoms) (H++ catalyst → H*); (ii) the generation of intermediate state (the adsorption of H*); (iii) the desorption of the product (1/2 H2) [70]. Thus, the intermediate state plays a crucial role in the evaluation of the photocatalytic hydrogen production capacity, and is usually measured with the Gibbs free energy (ΔGH*). Generally, the closer the absolute value of ΔGH* is to 0, the better the hydrogen production activity is [71]. As can be seen from Fig. 5e, the ΔGH* values for CCN (−4.12 eV) and CDs/CCN (−2.53 eV) are significantly reduced compared to that of CN (−6.08 eV), demonstrating that both C-doping and CDs loading could effectively optimize the adsorption of intermediates, desorption of products and promote the activation of protons. For further validation, the overpotentials of the as-prepared samples for H2 evolution were examined by linear scanning voltammetry (LSV) curves, and the results are displayed in Fig. 5f. The overpotential required for CN can reach −1.34 V at 10 mA cm−2, while CCN-5 and 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 can significantly reduce to −1.2 and −1.14 V, indicating that C-doping and CDs loading in CN can jointly overcome the kinetic barriers and improve the reaction kinetics of photocatalytic H2 production.

To investigate the specific relationship between the carbon doping and carbon loading in CN modification and the interfacial charge transfer dynamics, transient photovoltage (TPV) test were performed. Similar to Fig. 5c, the photovoltage intensity (Fig. 6 a) of 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 and CCN-5 is significantly higher than that of CN, indicating that both C atomic doping and the introduction of CDs can lead to the continuous improvement of photo-induced carrier separation capacity for CN, which is consistent with the results of PL (Fig. 5a). The maximum charge extraction time and the maximum charge extraction number of as-prepared samples were obtained by local amplification and area integration of the original TPV curves (Fig. 6b and c). As exhibited in Fig. 6b, the maximum charge extraction time of CCN-5 (tmax2) has been significantly shortened compared with CN (tmax1), illustrating that C atomic doping can accelerate the process of electron transport and improve the internal electron transfer efficiency [72]. It is noteworthy that after the loading CDs on the CCN, the maximum charge extraction time of 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 (tmax3) and CCN-5 (tmax2) was essentially identical, indicating that CDs play little role in charge extraction. Combined with Fig. 6c, the maximum charge extraction amount of 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 (A3) is still increased compared with that of CCN-5 (A2) after the introduction of CDs, which can be inferred that the role of CDs in the system is to act as receptors for holes, reducing the recombination rate of photo-generated carriers through the efficient extraction of holes in order to facilitate the proton reduction process. In general, the electron lifetime of the photocatalyst was evaluated by using the electron decay constant of the sample [73]. As exhibited in Fig. 6d, the electron decay constant of CCN-5 (τ2 = 2.000 ms) is higher than that of CN (τ1 = 1.103 ms) and 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 (τ3 = 1.737 ms), which indicates that C atomic doping in the network of CN can form a trap state to capture electron and strengthen the volume charge transfer of CN, which is the main reason for reducing the recombination rate of electron-hole pairs. However, after the introduction of CDs, the electron lifetime of 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 was reduced to some extent, which may be related to the fact that the main role for the introduction of CDs increases the surface-active sites, shortens the distance of the interface electron transfer, and enhances the rate of the electronic interface transfer of CCN [74]. In order to facilitate the comparison of the photocatalytic performance of CN, CCN-5 and 0.5-CDs/CCN-5, the surface effective electron numbers of each sample were calculated using the formula (ne = A*τ/tmax) and listed in Table S6. According to the calculation results, C atomic doping reduces the bandgap of CN and achieves excellent electron extraction performance. Although CDs have a weak effect on capturing electrons, holes can be extracted to promote the progress of the half-reaction over the holes during the photocatalysis. Consequently, the effective electron number on the surface of 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 has been improved in comparison of CCN-5, which is also an important aspect for the improvement of photocatalytic H2 evolution performance.

Generally, the separation and migration of photo-generated charges generally occur at a faster time scale (ps-ns), which is a prerequisite for the surface reaction (ms-s), thus transient absorption spectroscopy (TAS) was carried out to track in real time the relaxation dynamics of photo-generated charge carriers in CDs/CCN system from ultrafast time scale [75]. The TAS measurements under the pump excitation of 343 nm were carried out, and the results were displayed in Fig. 7 . The pseudo-colored TAS spectra of CN, CCN-5 and 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 tested in deionized water are exhibited in Fig. 7a-c, it is intuitively observed that the signals of all samples are detected around 850 nm. Among them, the signals of CCN-5 are significantly stronger than 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 and CN, while the signals of CN are extremely weak, which can be preliminarily judged that the carriers’ movement of CCN-5 is the most active upon photoexcitation, meaning that CCN-5 have the longest electronic relaxation process [76]. The TAS spectra with different delay times over CN, CCN-5 and 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 photocatalysts in Fig. 7e, f and g display the same trend that a negative signal was observed immediately after the pump excitation, and a positive signal began to appear at around 700 nm. Previous reports have been confirmed that the generation of negative signals is correlated with signals from ground-state bleaching (GSB) and/or stimulated emission (SE) [77]. Considering that the signal starts at around 650 nm, it overlaps with PL spectra of all samples in Fig. 5a (450 nm-700 nm). According to the characteristic of stimulated emission is the production of fluorescence, it can be judged that the generation of the negative signal is related to SE, which can reflect the recombination process of photo-induced carriers and/or bound excitons [78]. Notably, the SE signal range of CCN-5 (Fig. 7f, 650–690 nm) and 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 (Fig. 7g, 650–700 nm) was significantly narrowed and highly suppressed compared to that of CN (Fig. 7e, 650–750 nm), another evidence providing for its efficient charge carrier separation. Ordinarily, the discussion of positive signals corresponds to excited-state absorption (ESA) of photo-induced carriers (electrons and/or holes) [79]. The carrier relaxation time of CCN-5 (1.67 ps-843.37 ps) and 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 (1.67 ps-321.09 ps) relative to the pristine CN (1.67 ps-23.87 ps) expresses greatly extended, consistent with the results measured by TPV and TRPL, indicating that the photo-generated electrons are more likely to trigger photocatalytic reaction. To get a clearer understanding on the detailed features of the differences in carriers’ activity, the carrier dynamics curves at 800 nm (Fig. 7i, j, k and l) were extracted and fitted, and the fitting parameters are listed in Table 1 . A biexponential decay function was used to describe the carriers’ dynamics of pristine CN, and these two fitted components τ1 (96.46 ps) and τ2 (870.91 ps) of CN can be attributed to the shallow trapping of electron and the recombination of shallow trap state electron, respectively [80]. Different from the bare CN, the carrier dynamics curves of CCN-5 and 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 can be fitted suitably by the triple-exponential decay function [81]. and the τ1 and τ2 components correspond to relaxation processes from CB to different trap states, and τ3 corresponds to the recombination of photo-generated carriers [82]. Thus, the τ1 (96.46 ps) and τ2 (870.91 ps) components reveal that the shallow trapped electrons of pristine CN are relatively few and can be rapidly consumed by recombining with the holes on VB quickly before the surface catalytic reaction, which is obviously unfavorable for the hydrogen production process [83]. For the CCN-5 sample, the decrease of the τ1 component (54.15 ps) apparently suggests that the intermediate state capture decay of photo-generated electrons is faster, which may be related to the rapid radiative decay of shallow trap electrons induced by C-atomic doping [84]. Furthermore, both the τ1 (32.19 ps) and τ2 (218.1 ps) of 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 were further decreased after the introduction of CDs, which is due to the extra proton reduction reaction between H+ photogenerated by CDs and active shallow trap-state electrons [85]. In addition, the extraction effect of CDs for holes extended the recombination time of charge carriers, which can be reflected from the τ3 components of 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 (1734 ps) and CCN-5 (896.43 ps). To discuss in detail the effects of interfacial charge transfer and transport dynamics insight during the photocatalytic reaction with the addition of the sacrificial agent, the TAS measurements of 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 were conducted in deionized water with added 10 vol% TEOA. Like the testing system in deionized water, the negative and positive signals of the TAS spectra (Fig. 7d and h) over 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 sample are attributed to SE and ESA, respectively. Notably, it is clearly observed from the TAS spectra that the negative signals of SE are weaker, but the positive signals of ESA are stronger after addition of TEOA during the photocatalysis, indicating that the former is due to the oxidation of holes by the sacrificial agent reduces the recombination behavior of photo-generated carriers, while the latter implies that 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 achieves a longer carrier relaxation time, possibly due to the addition of the sacrificial agent affecting the extent of electron capture. Compared with conditions without a sacrificial agent, the fitted τ1 (τ1 = 5.14 ps) and τ2 (τ2 = 80.85 ps) of 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 decreased dramatically after the addition of TEOA, indicates that the introduction of sacrificial agent can facilitate the rapid transformation from deep to shallow trapping state of photogenerated electrons. In addition, the lifetime of the 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 composite (τ3 = 98188.63 ps) has been increased in comparison of the CCN-5 (τ3 = 51272.56 ps) under the conditions of adding TEOA (Fig. S17 and Table S7), implying that the holes from CN can be easily extracted and transported to the surface of CDs for enhancing the photocatalytic oxidation of TEOA, which effectively inhibits the utilization of deep trapped electrons while promotes participation of shallow trapped electrons during the photocatalysis.

The working principle of photocatalysis for the H2 production with CN modified by carbon doping and carbon loading is proposed in Fig. 8 . When CDs/CCN is irradiated with solar light, photons are absorbed to produce photo-induced charges (e− and h+ ), which are injected into the CB and VB of photocatalyst. Due to the electron capture effect of C atoms doping, the C atoms around the doped C atoms in CCN can effectively capture the surrounding photo-generated electrons, which is easier to form the electron enrichment region, and then realize the rapid and directional migration and volume-phase separation of CN. Besides, C doping induces the formation of shallow trap states (STS) to trap the active electrons on the CB and inhibits the reorganization process of photo-generated electrons, optimizes the adsorption of intermediate states around C atoms, product desorption, and promotes H2 production through proton activation. The addition of sacrificial agents can promote the rapid transition of inactive electrons from the deep trapped state (DTS) to the shallow trap state (STS), which also promotes the proton reduction reaction. DFT and TPV results have confirmed that the fast holes’ transfer path established between CDs and CCN make the CDs possess the extractive effect for the holes for the oxidation reaction with TEOA, further achieving spatial separation of electron-hole pairs.

In summary, we developed a dual-carbon modified g-C3N4 system via a thermal polymerization strategy by introducing the gelatin and CDs for photocatalytic water splitting into H2 production. The optimal 0.5-CDs/CCN-5 exhibits a significantly improved photocatalytic H2 evolution rate of 8682 μmol h−1 g−1 under full-spectrum light irradiation, which is around 4 times higher than that of pure CN (2106 μmol h−1 g−1). The main reason for the high-efficient activity of the composite system belongs to the role of carbon doping and carbon loading: (i) the doping of C atoms in CN localized the electrons and realized the directional extraction of electrons, and the formation of shallow trap states can be induced to capture active electrons, thus inhibiting the generation of deep trap states and direct reorganization of photo-generated electrons; (ii) the loading of the CDs on the CCN surface establishes a fast hole transfer path while providing additional reaction sites for the sacrificial agent oxidation by the extracted holes, greatly improving the efficiency of surface separation of electron-hole pairs over composite system. This work presents an in-depth understanding of the carrier dynamics of carbon doping and carbon loading in semiconductor, which could provide reference value for the design and modification of carbon-based photocatalytic materials.
